$comment: Material Detective cases, Material Match pairs, and parent-approved experiments. Every clue must be scientifically true. answers reference element atomic numbers where relevant. Claims verified 2026-07-06; citations in docs/SOURCES.md.

clues: I hide inside the walls of your house.
I'm a shiny orange-brown metal.
Electricity zooms through me to power your lights.
detective.reveal: Copper (Cu, element 29) conducts electricity brilliantly and is cheap enough to wire every house on Earth.

clues: I'm invisible, but I love going UP.
I'm lighter than air and I never catch fire.
You'll find me at birthday parties, tied to a string.
detective.reveal: Helium (He, element 2) is lighter than air, so balloons filled with it float — and being a noble gas, it can't burn.

clues: People call me 'lead', but that's not what I am at all.
I'm made of carbon in soft, slippery layers.
I leave a gray trail when you drag me across paper.
detective.reveal: Graphite is carbon (C, element 6) arranged in flat sheets that slide off onto paper. Diamond is the same carbon arranged differently!

clues: I'm made of two dangerous elements that become harmless together.
My crystals are tiny perfect cubes — check with a magnifying glass!
You shake me onto french fries.
detective.reveal: Salt is sodium (Na, 11) + chlorine (Cl, 17). Alone they're hazardous; bonded together they're dinner-table safe.

clues: I'm a metal, but I'm so thin you can tear me with your fingers.
I wrap sandwiches and cover leftovers.
My big brothers are soda cans and airplane wings.
detective.reveal: Aluminum (Al, element 13) is soft enough to roll into super-thin foil, yet strong enough to build planes from.

clues: A magnet grabs me instantly.
Leave me out in the rain and I slowly turn orange and crumbly.
A hammer is my best friend.
detective.reveal: Steel is mostly iron (Fe, element 26). Iron is magnetic, and rust is iron reacting with oxygen and water.

clues: I started life as ordinary sand.
I'm a 'semiconductor' — I can switch electricity on and off super fast.
Every phone, tablet, and game console has me as its brain.
detective.reveal: Chips are made from silicon (Si, element 14), purified from sand into perfect crystals that do billions of on/off switches per second.

clues: I've been shiny for a thousand years and I'll stay shiny for a thousand more.
I'm soft for a metal — you could dent me with a good bite.
Pirates buried chests full of me.
detective.reveal: Gold (Au, element 79) never rusts or tarnishes — that's why treasure stays treasure.

clues: I'm a tiny coiled wire that glows white-hot.
I survive temperatures that would melt almost any other metal.
Flip a switch and I light up the room (in old-style bulbs).
detective.reveal: Filaments are tungsten (W, element 74) — the metal with the highest melting point of all: 3,422 °C.

clues: I'm made by melting beach sand until it flows like honey.
Light passes straight through me.
You look through me every day, but please don't throw balls at me.
detective.reveal: Glass is melted sand — mostly silicon (Si, 14) and oxygen (O, 8). Cooled fast, the atoms freeze in place before forming crystals.

clues: I'm hiding inside your earbuds and phone, making sound and buzz alerts.
I'm the strongest kind of permanent magnet ever made.
I team up with iron and boron to get my mighty grip.
detective.reveal: Neodymium (Nd, element 60) makes the strongest permanent magnets in the world — every earbud, phone speaker, and electric car motor depends on them.

clues: I store the energy that keeps a phone alive all day.
I'm built around the lightest metal of all.
Plug in the charger at night and I fill back up.
detective.reveal: Rechargeable phone batteries are lithium batteries — lithium (Li, element 3) is the lightest metal and carries a mighty charge for its weight.

clues: I grow into rainbow-colored staircase crystals.
My element is also the secret ingredient in pink tummy-soothing medicine.
I'm the gentle giant at the heavy end of the periodic table.
detective.reveal: Bismuth (Bi, element 83) grows 'hopper' crystals with rainbow oxide skins — and its compounds calm upset stomachs.

clues: I only glow when electricity runs through me.
My light is always red-orange — other colors come from my gas cousins.
You'll spot me shining in shop windows at night.
detective.reveal: True neon signs glow red-orange: that's neon gas (Ne, element 10) lighting up when electricity excites its atoms.

clues: Teachers drag me across blackboards.
I'm made of the same stuff as seashells and limestone cliffs.
My main element also builds your bones and teeth.
detective.reveal: Chalk is calcium carbonate — the same calcium (Ca, element 20) compound that forms seashells, limestone, and eggshells.

clues: I'm a curvy yellow fruit famous for one particular element.
My special element helps your nerves send messages.
That element's symbol is K, from its old Latin name.
detective.reveal: Bananas are famously rich in potassium (K, element 19, from 'kalium') — the element your nerves and muscles use to send signals.

clues: Take a sniff at the swimming pool — that's me on the job.
I destroy germs in the water so it's safe to swim.
Teamed up with sodium, I sit on your dinner table instead.
detective.reveal: Pool water is kept safe by chlorine (Cl, element 17) — fierce on germs, yet as sodium chloride it's ordinary table salt.

clues: Inside antique thermometers, I rose up the tube when you had a fever.
I'm the only metal that's liquid at room temperature.
I'm toxic, so modern thermometers replaced me.
detective.reveal: Old thermometers used mercury (Hg, element 80), the only room-temperature liquid metal. Safer liquids and electronics do the job today.

clues: Doctors put me inside people — as new hips and tooth roots.
I'm as strong as steel but much lighter.
I'm named after the giants of Greek myths.
detective.reveal: Replacement hips and dental implants are titanium (Ti, element 22): strong, light, rust-proof, and friendly to the human body.

clues: I'm in every breath you take — about 1 of every 100 parts of air — but I never react.
My name means 'the lazy one' in Greek.
Welders spray me over hot metal to keep the air away.
detective.reveal: Welders shield hot metal with argon (Ar, element 18) — the 'lazy' noble gas that refuses to react, so the weld stays clean.

clues: I sit on your ceiling, keeping watch day and night.
A sealed speck of a lab-made element helps me sense smoke instantly.
When I start chirping, ask a grown-up to change my battery!
detective.reveal: Many smoke detectors hold a tiny sealed speck of americium (Am, element 95) — a human-made element quietly guarding millions of homes.

clues: I'm the dull-gray protective coat on buckets, fences, and crash barriers.
I take the rust hit myself so the steel underneath stays safe.
Mix my element with copper and you get brass.
detective.reveal: Galvanized steel wears a zinc jacket (Zn, element 30). Zinc corrodes first — a bodyguard sacrificing itself for the iron below.

clues: I burn with a light so white and bright it can hurt your eyes.
Sailors fire me into the sky in emergencies.
Green plants keep my element at the heart of their chlorophyll.
detective.reveal: Emergency flares and old camera flashes burn magnesium (Mg, element 12) — the same element every green leaf needs to catch sunlight.

clues: I'm the most exact timekeeper humans have ever built.
The official second is defined by my atoms vibrating exactly 9,192,631,770 times.
GPS satellites carry my cousins so your map app knows where you are.
detective.reveal: Atomic clocks count the vibrations of cesium atoms (Cs, element 55) — the world's definition of one second, and the heartbeat of GPS.

experiments.title: Liquid Rainbow Tower
experiments.teaches: Density — why heavy liquids sink and light ones float
experiments.safety: Kitchen ingredients only. Ask a grown-up to help pour.
materials: Water (add food coloring if you like)
steps: Pour honey slowly into the middle of the glass.
Gently pour water on top — pour it over the back of a spoon so it lands softly.
Now gently pour the oil the same way.
Watch: three layers! Honey is the densest so it sinks; oil is the least dense so it floats.
experiments.science: Density means how much stuff is packed into a space. Elements work the same way: lead is super dense, aluminum is not — that's why planes are aluminum!

experiments.title: Grow Your Own Crystals
experiments.teaches: Crystal structures — atoms stacking in patterns
experiments.safety: Hot water needs a grown-up. Don't drink the salt water.
steps: Have a grown-up fill the jar with warm water.
Stir in salt, one spoon at a time, until no more dissolves.
Tie a string to a pencil and rest the pencil across the jar so the string hangs in the water.
Wait a few days. Tiny cube-shaped crystals will grow on the string!
experiments.science: Salt crystals are cubes because sodium and chlorine atoms stack in a cubic pattern — the same pattern shown in the app's crystal viewer!

experiments.title: Balloon Hair Magic
experiments.teaches: Static electricity — electrons jumping between materials
materials: Your hair (clean and dry works best)
steps: Rub the balloon on your hair about 10 times.
Slowly lift the balloon — your hair reaches up toward it!
Hold the balloon near tiny paper bits and watch them jump.
experiments.science: Rubbing moves tiny particles called electrons onto the balloon. Electrons are part of every atom of every element — and moving electrons are what electricity is!

experiments.title: Penny Polish Lab
experiments.safety: Vinegar is safe but don't drink the mixture. Wash hands after.
steps: Mix a splash of vinegar and a teaspoon of salt in the bowl.
Drop in the dull pennies and count to 30.
Take them out, rinse with water, and dry them. Shiny!
experiments.science: Dull pennies are coated in copper oxide — copper that grabbed oxygen from the air. Vinegar + salt dissolves that layer, uncovering the bright copper underneath.

experiments.title: Iron in Your Breakfast
experiments.teaches: Real iron is really in your food
experiments.safety: Safe. Don't eat the mushed cereal afterward.
materials: Iron-fortified breakfast cereal (check the label for iron)
steps: Crush a cup of cereal into powder in the bag.
Add water and squish it into a soup. Let it sit 20 minutes.
Hold the magnet against the outside of the bag and swirl slowly.
Look closely at the magnet spot: tiny dark specks — that's real iron!
experiments.science: Cereal makers add tiny particles of actual iron (Fe, element 26) because your blood needs it to carry oxygen. Iron is magnetic, so a magnet can pull it out.

experiments.title: The Great Rust Race
experiments.teaches: Corrosion — and how engineers stop it
experiments.safety: Handle nails carefully. Ask a grown-up to set it up.
steps: Cup 1: nail in water. Cup 2: nail coated in oil, then in water. Cup 3: dry nail, no water.
Check the nails every day for a week.
Which nail rusts first? Which stays shiny?
experiments.science: Rust needs iron + water + oxygen. The oil coat blocks water and oxygen — exactly like the zinc coating engineers put on real steel (galvanizing). This is material engineering: stopping nature from eating your bridge!
